title: Normalized Coordinates
concept: Normalized coordinates represent bounding box locations as fractions of the image's width and height, keeping them scale-independent.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How many coordinate numbers are needed to represent a bounding box?
exercises.explain: A bounding box is always represented by 4 numbers (either corners or center + size).

cards.title: Resizing a Photo
cards.body: If you define a face box as pixel 100 to 200 in a photo, what happens if you shrink the photo by half? The face is now at pixels 50 to 100! Your hardcoded pixel coordinates are broken. We need a system that scales automatically.

cards.title: What are Normalized Coordinates?
cards.body: Normalized Coordinates express bounding box values as fractions between 0.0 and 1.0 relative to the image width and height. To convert to pixels, multiply the normalized values by the image dimensions.

cards.title: Relative vs. Absolute
cards.body: If you are reading a book, saying 'I am on line 50' is absolute (like pixels). Saying 'I am halfway through the chapter' is relative (normalized). No matter if the book has large or small print, 'halfway' is always in the same place!

cards.title: Doing the Math
cards.body: In a 1000x1000 pixel image, a box at xmin=200, ymin=300, xmax=500, ymax=600 is normalized to [0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 0.6] by dividing all x coordinates by 1000, and y coordinates by 1000.

cards.title: Normalized does not mean harder
cards.body: You might think using decimals makes the calculations harder. However, it makes training neural networks much easier because the network doesn't have to deal with massive pixel variations across different image sizes.

cards.title: Pixel vs. Normalized
cards.body: Comparing pixel-based boxes vs normalized boxes when an image is resized:

exercises.prompt: Why do deep learning models prefer normalized coordinates over pixel coordinates?
exercises.explain: Normalized coordinates are scale-independent, meaning they remain valid even if the image is resized.

options.text: They allow the model to handle images of any size without recalculating coordinates
They make the bounding boxes circular
They convert the color images to grayscale automatically
They speed up the camera's shutter speed

exercises.prompt: True or False: A normalized coordinate of 0.5 along the x-axis represents the exact horizontal center of the image.
exercises.explain: True. Normalized values range from 0.0 to 1.0, so 0.5 represents the midpoint (50%) of the image dimension.
Multiplying the normalized fraction (e.g., 0.2) by the total width or height yields the pixel coordinate.
exercises.sentence: To convert a normalized coordinate to pixels, we ___ the normalized value by the image dimension.

exercises.prompt: Which bounding box is represented in normalized coordinates?
exercises.explain: Coordinates represented as decimals between 0.0 and 1.0 indicate normalized coordinates.

exercises.prompt: You are processing an image of size 800x600 pixels. A predicted bounding box in normalized corner format is [0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 0.8]. What is the bottom-right corner pixel coordinate (xmax, ymax)?
exercises.explain: Exactly! The normalized xmax is 0.5, so 0.5 * 800 (width) = 400 pixels. The normalized ymax is 0.8, so 0.8 * 600 (height) = 480 pixels. Next: let's learn how we measure how close our predicted boxes are to the truth!
